title: The fundamental economic problem
summary:
## The big idea Economics begins with one stubborn fact: we can never have everything we want. Human **wants are effectively unlimited**, but the **resources** to satisfy them — time, land, workers, machines, money — are **limited**. That clash between limitless wants and limited means is the **fundamental economic problem**, and its proper name is **scarcity**. Because resources are scarce, everyone — a person, a firm, a whole government — is forced to make **choices**. And here is the idea that runs through the entire course: **every choice means giving something up**. ## Scarcity, choice and opportunity cost These three ideas form a chain. **Scarcity** forces **choice**, and every choice has an **opportunity cost** — the *next best alternative you give up*. Notice the wording: opportunity cost is the single best thing foregone, not ‘everything else’. If a government spends its budget on a new hospital, the opportunity cost is the *one* next best thing that money could have done — say a new school — not every possible project at once. Opportunity cost applies to everyone. A student choosing to revise gives up an evening out; a firm building a new factory gives up the interest it could have earned on the money. Thinking in terms of opportunity cost is what separates an economist's reasoning from everyday reasoning. ## The three questions every economy must answer However rich or poor, every economy has to solve the same three problems: **what** to produce, **how** to produce it, and **for whom** to produce (how output is shared out). Different economies answer these questions in different ways — through markets, through the state, or a mix — which is the subject of later chapters. ## Who makes the choices, and why Three **economic agents** make decisions, each with its own aim. **Consumers (households)** try to maximise their **utility** (satisfaction). **Firms** try to maximise **profit**. **Governments** try to maximise **social welfare** — the wellbeing of society as a whole. A useful habit is to think **at the margin**: economists usually ask not ‘all or nothing?’ but ‘is *one more* unit worth it?’ — comparing the extra benefit with the extra cost. ## Free goods and economic goods A **free good** has *no* opportunity cost because it is not scarce — the classic example is the air we breathe. An **economic good** is scarce, so producing or consuming it uses up resources that had other uses; it therefore has an opportunity cost. Almost everything we buy is an economic good — even ‘free’ things like a ‘free’ app usually have a cost to someone. ## Positive versus normative Economists constantly separate facts from opinions. A **positive statement** is factual and can, in principle, be **tested** against evidence — for example, ‘Japan's inflation rate was about 3% in 2023.’ A **normative statement** is a **value judgement** and often contains words like *should* or *ought* — for example, ‘the government should cut fuel taxes.’ You cannot prove a normative statement true or false; people disagree about it because they hold different values. Spotting the difference matters: many policy arguments are really disagreements about values, not facts. ## Economics as a social science Economics is a **social science** — it studies human behaviour using models. Because the real world is complicated, economists isolate one thing at a time using the assumption **ceteris paribus** (‘all other things equal’). The subject splits into **microeconomics** (individual markets, consumers and firms) and **macroeconomics** (the whole economy — growth, inflation, unemployment). ## Real-world application Scarcity is everywhere. A government facing a limited budget must choose between, say, defence and healthcare — real trade-offs debated in the USA, India and across Europe. During the global energy shortage of 2022, scarce gas had to be rationed and prioritised, a vivid reminder that even developed economies cannot escape the fundamental problem.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Scarcity** = unlimited wants vs limited resources — the fundamental economic problem. - Scarcity → **choice** → **opportunity cost** (the *single next best* alternative foregone). - Every economy must answer **what, how and for whom** to produce. - Agents & aims: consumers → **utility**, firms → **profit**, government → **social welfare**; think **at the margin**. - **Free good** = no opportunity cost; **economic good** = scarce, has an opportunity cost. - **Positive** = testable fact; **normative** = value judgement (‘should/ought’). - Economics is a **social science** using **ceteris paribus**; split into **micro** and **macro**.
revisionNotes:
# The economic problem — quick revision

**Core chain**
- **Scarcity** (unlimited wants, limited resources) → forces **choice** → every choice has an **opportunity cost** = the *next best* alternative given up.

**Three questions**
- Every economy answers **what**, **how** and **for whom** to produce.

**Agents and their goals**
- Consumers → maximise **utility**; firms → maximise **profit**; government → maximise **social welfare**.
- Think **at the margin** (is *one more* unit worth it?).

**Types of good**
- **Free good** — not scarce, no opportunity cost (e.g. air).
- **Economic good** — scarce, has an opportunity cost.

**Positive vs normative**
- **Positive** = factual, testable. **Normative** = value judgement (‘should/ought’).

**The subject**
- A **social science** using **ceteris paribus**; **micro** (markets/firms) vs **macro** (whole economy).
questions:
# Practice questions — The fundamental economic problem **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in any chapter's Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** Which of the following is the best definition of opportunity cost? - A — the money price paid for a good - B — the next best alternative given up when a choice is made - C — the total of all alternatives given up - D — the cost of producing one more unit
▸ Show answer

B. Opportunity cost is the single *next best* alternative foregone — not the money price (A) and not everything else (C).

**2.** Which of these is a normative statement? - A — unemployment rose to 6% last year - B — a rise in income tax reduced consumer spending - C — the government ought to spend more on healthcare - D — inflation was higher in 2022 than in 2021
▸ Show answer

C. ‘Ought to’ signals a value judgement (normative). The others are factual/testable (positive).

**3.** A good is a *free good* if: - A — it is provided by the government at no charge - B — it has no opportunity cost because it is not scarce - C — its price is zero in the market - D — it is a merit good
▸ Show answer

B. A free good is not scarce, so using it involves no opportunity cost (e.g. air). A government-provided or zero-price good still uses scarce resources.

**4.** The existence of opportunity cost is a direct result of: - A — inflation - B — government intervention - C — scarcity - D — international trade
▸ Show answer

C. Because resources are scarce we must choose, and choosing means giving up an alternative — that is opportunity cost.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A government has a fixed health budget for the year. It is deciding between funding a new hospital or a nationwide vaccination programme. Officials note that ‘every pound spent on one is a pound not spent on the other’. **(a)** Define ‘opportunity cost’. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, using the example above, why the government faces an opportunity cost. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse how scarcity forces choices on a government. **[6]** **(d)** ‘Decisions about government spending should be based only on positive economic analysis.’ Assess this view. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The next best alternative foregone when a choice is made. [1 + 1 development]

(b) The budget is fixed (scarce); choosing the hospital means giving up the vaccination programme — that foregone programme is the opportunity cost. Apply to the data.

(c) Chain: limited budget → cannot fund everything → must rank options → choosing one sacrifices others → opportunity cost. Reward developed reasoning.

(d) For: positive analysis gives objective evidence (costs, lives saved). Against: spending also involves value judgements about priorities and fairness (normative), which positive analysis cannot settle. Judgement: both are needed.

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain, with examples, the difference between a positive and a normative statement. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether the concept of opportunity cost is useful for a firm deciding whether to build a new factory. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Positive = factual, testable; normative = value judgement (‘should/ought’). Give a clear example of each and explain why one can be tested and the other cannot.

(b) Useful: highlights the sacrificed alternative (e.g. interest foregone, other projects) so resources are used where the return is highest. Limits: hard to measure, ignores non-financial factors, uncertainty about the future. Judgement required.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain why scarcity means every economy must answer the questions ‘what, how and for whom to produce’. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether consumers, firms and governments all pursue the same objective. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Resources are insufficient for all wants, so society must decide which goods to make, the method of production, and how to distribute output. Link each question to scarcity.

(b) No: consumers maximise utility, firms maximise profit, governments maximise social welfare — these can conflict (e.g. profit vs welfare). But some overlap (all respond to incentives). Reach a judgement.

keyTerms.definition: The fundamental economic problem: unlimited wants exceed the limited resources available to satisfy them.
The next best alternative foregone when a choice is made.
A good that is scarce and therefore has an opportunity cost.
A good with no opportunity cost because it is not scarce (e.g. air).
An objective statement of fact that can be tested against evidence.
A subjective statement based on a value judgement (often uses ‘should/ought’).
‘All other things equal’ — holding everything else constant to study one variable.
The effect of one additional (marginal) unit; decisions weigh marginal benefit against marginal cost.

tips: Define **scarcity, choice and opportunity cost together** as a chain — examiners reward the link.
State opportunity cost as the **single next best** alternative, never ‘everything else’.
Spot **normative** language instantly: *should, ought, too high, unfair*.
Use **real, varied examples** (a government budget trade-off; rationing during the 2022 energy crisis).
Remember a **free good has zero opportunity cost** — most ‘free’ things are actually economic goods.
diagrams: No diagram is required here, but opportunity cost can be shown on a **PPC** (see the PPC chapter): moving along the curve, more of one good means less of the other.
